sections.content:

Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.

end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.

If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

Level 1: you have used 1 of about 18 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: SPACE was pressed and the board changed. Let me compare the before and after frames to see what changed. I need to look at the transition to understand what SPACE does.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after SPACE
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation

before_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in before['nodes']}
after_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in after['nodes']}

# Check what changed - compare node colors and positions
print("=== Before SPACE ===")
for n in before['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] < 100:  # Skip large background
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("\n=== After SPACE ===")
for n in after['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] < 100:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check for color changes on same-id nodes
print("\n=== Color changes ===")
for nid in before_nodes:
    if nid in after_nodes:
        bn = before_nodes[nid]
        an = after_nodes[nid]
        if bn['color'] != an['color']:
            print(f"  Node {nid}: {bn['color']} -> {an['color']}")
        if bn['boundary'] != an['boundary']:
            print(f"  Node {nid}: boundary changed")

# Check for new or removed nodes
before_ids = set(before_nodes.keys())
after_ids = set(after_nodes.keys())
print(f"\nRemoved nodes: {before_ids - after_ids}")
print(f"New nodes: {after_ids - before_ids}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"SPACE": "Causes board change - need to determine what"}, "object_taxonomy": {"top_panel": "4 colored squares (b, N, Y, p) in black frame", "center_panel": "red rectangle with 4 gray dots, white corner brackets", "bottom_panel": "4 colored squares (N, p, b, Y) in charcoal frame"}, "hud_map": {}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Level 1: Top has 4 colored squares [b, N, Y, p], center has red box with 4 gray dots, bottom has [N, p, b, Y].", "goal_model": "Need to understand SPACE effect first.", "recent_findings": ["SPACE causes board change"], "open_questions": ["What exactly did SPACE change?"], "current_plan": "Analyze the diff, then try MOUSE on different colored squares.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see what SPACE does:
1. The gray line (node 20) shrunk from 64 to 63 pixels - it's a timer/progress bar
2. The bottom panel colors shifted:
   - Before: [N, p, b, Y] (green, purple, blue, yellow)
   - After: [N, p, b, Y] but the node colors changed: N→c, p→N, b→p, Y→b
   - Wait, let me re-read: Node 22 was N→c, Node 23 was p→N, Node 24 was b→p, Node 25 was Y→b
   - So the colors shifted: the first became charcoal (background?), and each subsequent one took the previous color
   - New node 26 is Y at the end
So SPACE shifts the bottom row colors to the left, with the first one disappearing and a new one appearing at the end. But wait - looking more carefully:
- Before bottom: [N, p, b, Y] at positions [18, 26, 34, 42]
- After bottom: node 22 (pos 18) was N→c (became background), node 23 (pos 26) was p→N, node 24 (pos 34) was b→p, node 25 (pos 42) was Y→b, and new node 26 (pos 42) is Y
Wait, that's confusing. Let me look at boundaries:
- Node 22: [57, 18] - [60, 21] → was N, now c (charcoal = background)
- Node 23: [57, 26] - [60, 29] → was p, now N
- Node 24: [57, 34] - [60, 37] → was b, now p
- Node 25: [57, 42] - [60, 45] → was Y, now b
- Node 26: [57, 42] - [60, 45] → new, Y
Hmm, node 25 and 26 have the same boundary. That's odd. Maybe node 25 changed to b and node 26 is a new Y at the same position? That doesn't make sense for 4-connected objects.
Actually wait - let me re-read. Node 25 was Y at [57, 42]-[60, 45], after it's b at same position. Node 26 is Y at [57, 42]-[60, 45]. They can't both be at the same position...
Oh wait, the "after" listing shows node 25 as b and node 26 as Y. But node 25's boundary is [57, 42]-[60, 45] and node 26's is also [57, 42]-[60, 45]. This seems like the same cell. Maybe the segmentation is slightly different.
Actually, I think what happened is:
- The bottom row shifted left: N disappeared (became charcoal), p→N, b→p, Y→b
- And Y appeared at a new position (node 26)
But node 26 has the same boundary as node 25... Let me just look at this differently.
The pattern seems to be: SPACE shifts the bottom row colors left by one position. The leftmost color disappears, and a new color appears on the right.
The top row order is: [b, N, Y, p] (blue, green, yellow, purple)
The bottom row started as: [N, p, b, Y] (green, purple, blue, yellow)
After SPACE: the bottom became [c, N, p, b] with Y somewhere... 
Actually, I think the goal is to make the bottom row match the top row order [b, N, Y, p]. Currently the bottom is [N, p, b, Y] and SPACE shifts left.
Let me think about this as a permutation problem. The target is [b, N, Y, p] and current is [N, p, b, Y]. SPACE shifts left (first element drops, rest shift left, new element added at end).
But I don't know what element gets added. Let me try SPACE a few more times to see the pattern, or try MOUSE to interact with specific colored squares.
Actually, let me try MOUSE on the center gray dots or on the colored squares to see if that does something different. Let me first try clicking on one of the gray dots in the center.

sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# SPACE shifts bottom row colors left. Let me understand the pattern better.
# Before: bottom = [N, p, b, Y]
# After SPACE: first became charcoal (disappeared), rest shifted left, new Y appeared
# So it seems like a cyclic left shift: [N, p, b, Y] -> [p, b, Y, N] but the first slot became charcoal

# Let me try clicking on one of the gray dots in the center to see if MOUSE does something different
# The gray dots are at approximately rows 29-30, cols 22, 28, 34, 40
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 29, 'col': 22}])
print(f"MOUSE result: {last_action_result}")

# Quick check of what changed

        print(f"  color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
